summary: Gruber proposes that Apple should add a games subscription service as part of its upcoming content bundle alongside news and video. He envisions Apple partnering with indie iOS game developers to create exclusive titles, paying them based on engagement time rather than through in-app purchases. He argues that indie developers would welcome recurring revenue without exploitative monetization, and that games are better suited to the iPhone than TV content. The piece frames this as a natural extension of Apple's content investment strategy.
key_points: Apple should bundle a games subscription with its news and video offerings for a combined price
Indie game developers, not major publishers like EA or Epic, are the right partners because they value platform exclusivity and would welcome non-IAP revenue
Payment should be engagement-based — more play time means more revenue for developers — mirroring the proposed news payment model
Games are better suited to the iPhone than video content, making this a smarter investment for Apple's strongest platform
money_quotes: No in-app purchases — you pay a monthly fee to subscribe to Apple Games and that is it, you get it all.
Forget the big companies like Epic and EA — they're never going to go for platform exclusivity and they won't walk away from casino-style pay-to-win in-app purchase schemes.
I'll bet they'd jump at the chance for recurring revenue that doesn't come from shitty in-app-purchase game mechanics.
Games are way better suited to the iPhone, and the iPhone is Apple's strongest and most popular platform.
If Apple's going to spend billions on exclusive TV and movie content, why not spend money on exclusive games as well?
No money for clickbait headlines — Apple is looking for nourishing news and opinion pieces.
key_insight: Apple's content subscription strategy has a natural missing piece in indie games, which are better suited to the iPhone than video and could offer developers a welcome escape from exploitative in-app purchase economics.
